annotations.label: Convolution

anchor.snippet: convolution is a mathematical operation on two functions

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `convolution f g L μ x = ∫ t, L (f t) (g (x - t)) ∂μ` via a continuous bilinear map, generalizing the scalar convolution operation.

annotations.label: Relation to cross-correlation

anchor.snippet: differs from cross-correlation

annotations.note: Mathlib has no cross-correlation operator, so the comparison is not formalized.

annotations.label: Cross-correlation as adjoint (complex case)

anchor.snippet: the cross-correlation operator is the adjoint of the convolution operator

annotations.note: No cross-correlation operator or its adjoint relationship to convolution is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Deconvolution

anchor.snippet: Computing the inverse of the convolution operation is known as deconvolution

annotations.note: Deconvolution as an inversion operation is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Definition of convolution integral

anchor.snippet: It is defined as the integral of the product of the two functions

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.convolution` (with `convolution_def`) gives exactly `(f ⋆[L,μ] g) x = ∫ t, L (f t) (g (x - t)) ∂μ`.

annotations.label: Equivalent (commutative) definition

anchor.snippet: An equivalent definition is

annotations.note: `convolution_eq_swap` proves `(f ⋆[L,μ] g) x = ∫ t, L (f (x - t)) (g t) ∂μ`, the variable-substituted equivalent form.

annotations.label: Truncated convolution for one-sided support

anchor.snippet: the integration limits can be truncated

annotations.note: `posConvolution` defines the one-sided convolution `convolution (indicator (Ioi 0) f) (indicator (Ioi 0) g) L`, the truncated form for functions supported on `[0,∞)`.

annotations.label: Engineering notational convention

anchor.snippet: A common engineering notational convention is

annotations.note: This is a notational convention, not a mathematical statement, and is not represented in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Convolution via inverse Laplace transform

anchor.snippet: can be defined as the inverse Laplace transform of the product

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks the Laplace transform machinery needed to define convolution via inverse Laplace transform.

annotations.label: Convolution as LTI system output

anchor.snippet: an important class of operations known as linear time-invariant

annotations.note: Linear time-invariant systems theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Convolution with a unit impulse

anchor.snippet: the result of this process is simply

annotations.note: The identity property is captured for measures by `mconv_dirac_one` (μ ∗ₘ δ₁ = μ), but convolution of a function with a Dirac delta is not directly formalized.

annotations.label: Periodicity of convolution with periodic function

anchor.snippet: the convolution is also periodic and identical to

annotations.note: Circular/periodic convolution of functions and its periodicity are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Periodic summation

anchor.snippet: The summation is called a periodic summation of the function

annotations.note: The periodic summation (periodization) operator is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Circular (cyclic) convolution

anchor.snippet: is known as a circular or cyclic convolution of

annotations.note: The general monoid convolution specializes to a cyclic group, but circular convolution of periodic functions is not specifically formalized.

annotations.label: Periodic convolution

anchor.snippet: the operation is called a periodic convolution of

annotations.note: Periodic convolution of periodic functions over one period is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Discrete convolution

anchor.snippet: of integers, the discrete convolution of

annotations.note: `DiscreteConvolution.convolution` defines `(f ⋆[L] g) x = ∑' ab : fiber x, L (f a) (g b)` over a monoid, generalizing the integer discrete convolution.

annotations.label: Equivalent (commutative) discrete convolution

anchor.snippet: or equivalently (see commutativity ) by

annotations.note: `convolution_comm` proves `f ⋆[L] g = g ⋆[L] f` for symmetric bilinear maps over commutative monoids.

annotations.label: Cauchy product of polynomial coefficients

anchor.snippet: This is known as the Cauchy product of the coefficients

annotations.note: `Polynomial.coeff_mul` gives `coeff (p*q) n = ∑ x ∈ antidiagonal n, coeff p x.1 * coeff q x.2`, exactly the Cauchy product.

annotations.label: Finite summation for finite support

anchor.snippet: a finite summation may be used

annotations.note: `Polynomial.coeff_mul` realizes the finite-support discrete convolution as a finite antidiagonal sum, but the general truncation statement for finitely-supported sequences is not stated.

annotations.label: Periodicity of discrete convolution

anchor.snippet: the convolution is also periodic and identical to

annotations.note: Periodicity of circular discrete convolution is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Discrete periodic summation

anchor.snippet: is called a periodic summation of the function

annotations.note: The discrete periodic summation operator is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Circular discrete convolution

anchor.snippet: is known as a circular convolution of

annotations.note: The monoid discrete convolution covers convolution over a finite cyclic group, but circular discrete convolution is not specifically named or developed.

annotations.label: Cyclic convolution notation

anchor.snippet: for cyclic convolution denotes convolution over the cyclic group

annotations.note: Convolution over the cyclic group is an instance of `DiscreteConvolution.convolution` for a monoid `M`, though no dedicated cyclic-group notation exists.

annotations.label: Convolution on R^d

anchor.snippet: The convolution of two complex-valued functions on R d is itself a complex-valued function

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.convolution` is defined over any additive group with measure, of which complex-valued functions on `ℝ^d` are a special case.

annotations.label: Convolution of compactly supported continuous functions

anchor.snippet: If f and g are compactly supported continuous functions

annotations.note: `HasCompactSupport.convolution` proves the convolution of two compactly supported functions has compact support, with existence/continuity available alongside.

annotations.label: Compactly supported with locally integrable

anchor.snippet: if either function (say f ) is compactly supported and the other is locally integrable

annotations.note: `HasCompactSupport.convolutionExists_left` establishes the convolution is well-defined when one function is compactly supported continuous and the other locally integrable.

annotations.label: Convolution of locally square integrable one-sided functions

anchor.snippet: is also well defined when both functions are locally square integrable

annotations.note: `posConvolution` handles the one-sided (supported on `[0,∞)`) convolution, but the specific local-square-integrability well-definedness is not proven.

annotations.label: Convolution of L^1 functions is integrable

anchor.snippet: The convolution of f and g exists if f and g are both Lebesgue integrable functions

annotations.note: `Integrable.integrable_convolution` proves the convolution of two integrable functions is integrable.

annotations.label: L^1 convolved with L^p stays in L^p

anchor.snippet: Likewise, if f ∈ L 1 ( R d ) and g ∈ L p ( R d )

annotations.note: The file's own TODO notes the L^p/L^q convolution results are not yet done in Mathlib.

annotations.label: L^1 is a Banach algebra under convolution

anchor.snippet: L 1 is a Banach algebra under the convolution

annotations.note: No Banach algebra structure on L^1 under convolution is constructed in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Young's convolution inequality

anchor.snippet: Young's inequality implies that the convolution is a continuous bilinear map

annotations.note: Young's convolution inequality (L^p norm bounds) is absent; Mathlib's Young inequalities concern products `a^p/p + b^q/q`, not convolution.

annotations.label: Sharp Young inequality constant (Brascamp–Lieb)

anchor.snippet: there exists a constant B p , q < 1 such that

annotations.note: The sharp Brascamp–Lieb constant for Young's convolution inequality is not formalized.

annotations.label: Weak Young inequality estimate

anchor.snippet: A stronger estimate is true provided

annotations.note: The weak-type Young inequality estimate is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Convolution preserves rapid decay

anchor.snippet: if f and g are rapidly decreasing functions , then so is the convolution

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes closure under convolution for the full Schwartz class via `SchwartzMap.convolution`, but rapid decay alone (without smoothness) is not separately treated.

annotations.label: Schwartz functions closed under convolution

anchor.snippet: the class of Schwartz functions is closed under convolution

annotations.note: `SchwartzMap.convolution` produces a Schwartz function `𝓢(E,F₁) → 𝓢(E,F₂) →L 𝓢(E,F₃)`, showing the class is closed under convolution.

annotations.label: Convolution of a smooth compactly supported function with a distribution

anchor.snippet: If f is a smooth function that is compactly supported and g is a distribution

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks a theory of distributions (test-function duals) and convolution with them.

annotations.label: Associative law extended to distributions

anchor.snippet: remains valid in the case where f is a distribution, and g a compactly supported distribution

annotations.note: Associativity of convolution for distributions is not formalized since distribution convolution is absent.

annotations.label: Convolution of Borel measures

anchor.snippet: The convolution of any two Borel measures μ and ν of bounded variation is the measure

annotations.note: `Measure.conv` (additive `mconv`) is the pushforward of the product measure under addition, the convolution of two measures.

annotations.label: Young's inequality for measures

anchor.snippet: The convolution of measures also satisfies the following version of Young's inequality

annotations.note: The total-variation Young inequality `‖μ∗ν‖ ≤ ‖μ‖‖ν‖` is not formalized, though finiteness/probability preservation of convolutions is.

annotations.label: Integrable functions form a commutative associative algebra

anchor.snippet: is a commutative associative algebra without identity

annotations.note: The convolution algebra structure on L^1 is not constructed in Mathlib, though individual algebraic laws are proven separately.

anchors.snippet: is a commutative associative algebra without identity

annotations.label: Commutativity

anchor.snippet: Proof: By definition

annotations.note: `convolution_flip` proves `g ⋆[L.flip,μ] f = f ⋆[L,μ] g`, giving commutativity for symmetric bilinear maps such as scalar convolution.

annotations.label: Associativity

anchor.snippet: This follows from using Fubini's theorem

annotations.note: `convolution_assoc` proves `(f ⋆ g) ⋆ k = f ⋆ (g ⋆ k)` under suitable integrability conditions.

anchors.snippet: This follows from using Fubini's theorem

annotations.label: Distributivity

anchor.snippet: This follows from linearity of the integral

annotations.note: `ConvolutionExists.distrib_add` and `add_distrib` prove `f ⋆ (g+g') = f⋆g + f⋆g'` and the left-distributive analogue.

anchors.snippet: This follows from linearity of the integral

annotations.label: Associativity with scalar multiplication

anchor.snippet: for any real (or complex) number

annotations.note: `convolution_smul` and `smul_convolution` prove `a(f⋆g) = (a•f)⋆g = f⋆(a•g)`.

annotations.label: No multiplicative identity; delta is identity for distributions

anchor.snippet: No algebra of functions possesses an identity for the convolution

annotations.note: The Dirac measure acts as identity (`dirac_one_mconv`/`mconv_dirac_one`), but the no-function-identity statement is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: No algebra of functions possesses an identity for the convolution

annotations.label: Invertible distributions form an abelian group

anchor.snippet: Some distributions S have an inverse element S −1 for the convolution

annotations.note: Convolution-invertible distributions and their group structure are not formalized.

anchors.snippet: Some distributions S have an inverse element S −1 for the convolution

annotations.label: Property proven via the convolution theorem

anchor.snippet: Proof (using convolution theorem )

annotations.note: This is a meta-remark about proof technique rather than a distinct formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Integral of a convolution

anchor.snippet: the integral of their convolution on the whole space is simply obtained as the product of their integrals

annotations.note: `integral_convolution` proves `∫ (f ⋆[L,ν] g) ∂μ = L (∫ f) (∫ g)`.

annotations.label: Derivative of a convolution (one variable)

anchor.snippet: In the one-variable case

annotations.note: `hasDerivAt_convolution_right` proves `deriv (f ⋆ g) = f ⋆ (deriv g)` in the one-variable case.

annotations.label: Partial derivative of a convolution

anchor.snippet: an analogous formula holds with the partial derivative

annotations.note: `hasFDerivAt_convolution_right` computes the (Fréchet) derivative of the convolution as a convolution with the derivative, covering partial derivatives.

annotations.label: Convolution as a smoothing operation

anchor.snippet: differentiable as many times as f and g are in total

annotations.note: `contDiff_convolution_right` shows the convolution is `𝒞ⁿ` when one factor is `𝒞ⁿ` and compactly supported, but the sharp 'sum of regularities' statement is not formalized.

annotations.label: Difference operator on discrete convolution

anchor.snippet: the difference operator D f ( n ) = f ( n + 1) − f ( n ) satisfies an analogous relationship

annotations.note: The discrete difference operator's relation to discrete convolution is not formalized.

annotations.label: Convolution theorem

anchor.snippet: The convolution theorem states that

annotations.note: `Real.fourier_mul_convolution_eq` proves the Fourier transform of a convolution equals the product of the Fourier transforms (over Euclidean space).

annotations.label: Convolution theorem for other transforms

anchor.section: Convolution in other types of transformations
anchor.snippet: Versions of this theorem also hold for the Laplace transform

annotations.note: Convolution theorems for the Laplace and other transforms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Convolution via Fourier transform matrix

anchor.snippet: is the Fourier transform matrix

annotations.note: The DFT-matrix diagonalization of convolution is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalization to appropriate matrices

anchor.snippet: This can be generalized for appropriate matrices

annotations.note: The matrix generalization of the convolution-diagonalization identity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Convolution commutes with translations

anchor.snippet: The convolution commutes with translations

annotations.note: No lemma stating `τₓ(f ⋆ g) = (τₓ f) ⋆ g` exists in Mathlib's convolution file.

annotations.label: Translation operator

anchor.snippet: where τ x f is the translation of the function f by x

annotations.note: There is no named translation operator `τₓ f = f(· − x)` developed for convolution in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Schwartz function as convolution with translated delta

anchor.snippet: If f is a Schwartz function , then τ x f is the convolution with a translated Dirac delta function

annotations.note: Representing translation of a Schwartz function as convolution with a shifted Dirac delta is not formalized.

annotations.label: Translation-invariant operators are convolutions

anchor.snippet: Suppose that S is a bounded linear operator acting on functions which commutes with translations

annotations.note: The characterization of translation-invariant bounded operators as convolutions is not formalized.

annotations.label: Translation-invariant operators on L^1 as convolution with a Borel measure

anchor.snippet: every continuous translation invariant continuous linear operator on L 1 is the convolution with a finite Borel measure

annotations.note: The Wendel-type theorem for translation-invariant operators on L^1 is not formalized.

annotations.label: Convolution on a group

anchor.snippet: If G is a suitable group endowed with a measure λ

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.convolution` defines convolution on an additive (abelian) group with measure, a special case of the general (possibly non-abelian) group convolution.

annotations.label: Commutation with left translation

anchor.snippet: convolution with a fixed function g commutes with left translation

annotations.note: Commutation of group convolution with left translation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Convolution theorem on locally compact abelian groups

anchor.snippet: a version of the convolution theorem holds: the Fourier transform of a convolution is the pointwise product

annotations.note: The Pontryagin-dual convolution theorem on general locally compact abelian groups is not formalized (only the Euclidean case is).

annotations.label: Convolution operator T is compact

anchor.snippet: The operator T is compact

annotations.note: Compactness of the convolution operator on L^2 of a compact group is not formalized.

annotations.label: Adjoint of the convolution operator

anchor.snippet: its adjoint T* is convolution with

annotations.note: The adjoint of the convolution operator is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Convolution operator is normal

anchor.snippet: By the commutativity property cited above, T is normal

annotations.note: Normality of the convolution operator is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Simultaneous diagonalization by an orthonormal basis

anchor.snippet: there exists an orthonormal basis { h k } that simultaneously diagonalizes S

annotations.note: Simultaneous diagonalization of convolution operators by an orthonormal basis is not formalized for this setting.

annotations.label: Convolution on finite cyclic groups via circulant matrices

anchor.snippet: Convolution operators are here represented by circulant matrices

annotations.note: `Matrix.circulant` defines circulant matrices, but their identification with convolution operators on cyclic groups is not formalized.

annotations.label: Peter–Weyl basis for compact groups

anchor.snippet: the matrix coefficients of finite-dimensional unitary representations form an orthonormal basis in L 2 by the Peter–Weyl theorem

annotations.note: The Peter–Weyl theorem is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Convolution of Radon measures

anchor.snippet: If μ and ν are Radon measures on G , then their convolution μ ∗ ν is defined as the pushforward measure

annotations.note: `Measure.conv`/`mconv` is exactly the pushforward of the product measure under the group operation.

annotations.label: Total variation of convolution of measures

anchor.snippet: The convolution is also a Radon measure, whose total variation satisfies

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the convolution of finite measures is finite (and of probability measures is a probability measure), but not the sharp total-variation inequality.

annotations.label: Density of convolution of absolutely continuous measures

anchor.snippet: the convolution μ∗ν is also absolutely continuous, and its density function is just the convolution of the two separate density functions

annotations.note: `mconv_withDensity_eq_mlconvolution` proves `(μ.withDensity f) ∗ₘ (μ.withDensity g) = μ.withDensity (f ⋆ₘₗ g)`, i.e. the density is the convolution of densities.

annotations.label: Convolution of probability measures = distribution of sum

anchor.snippet: the convolution μ ∗ ν is the probability distribution of the sum X + Y of two independent random variables

annotations.note: `IndepFun.map_add_eq_map_conv_map₀` proves the law of `X+Y` for independent `X,Y` equals the convolution of their laws.

annotations.label: Infimal convolution

anchor.snippet: the infimal convolution of proper

annotations.note: Infimal convolution of convex functions is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Infimal convolution of convex functions is convex

anchor.snippet: the infimal convolution of convex functions is convex

annotations.note: Without an infimal convolution definition, its convexity is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: the infimal convolution of convex functions is convex

annotations.label: Legendre transform identity for infimal convolution

anchor.snippet: with the role of the Fourier transform is played instead by the Legendre transform

annotations.note: Neither the Legendre/Fenchel conjugate nor its interaction with infimal convolution is formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: with the role of the Fourier transform is played instead by the Legendre transform

annotations.label: Convolution on a bialgebra endomorphism algebra

anchor.snippet: the convolution φ ∗ ψ is defined as the composition

annotations.note: `LinearMap.convMul` defines the convolution product `f * g = mul' ∘ (f ⊗ g) ∘ comul` on linear maps from a coalgebra to an algebra.

annotations.label: Hopf algebra characterized by antipode

anchor.snippet: A bialgebra is a Hopf algebra if and only if it has an antipode

annotations.note: Mathlib's `HopfAlgebra` is defined as a bialgebra equipped with an antipode satisfying the convolution-inverse axioms, but no explicit 'bialgebra is Hopf iff antipode exists' iff-theorem is stated.

annotations.label: Convolutional neural networks

anchor.snippet: Convolutional neural networks apply multiple cascaded convolution kernels

annotations.note: Convolutional neural networks are an application, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Out-of-focus photograph (bokeh)

anchor.snippet: an out-of-focus photograph is a convolution of the sharp image with a lens function

annotations.note: This imaging application is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reverberation in acoustics

anchor.snippet: reverberation is the convolution of the original sound with echoes

annotations.note: This acoustics application is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Weighted moving average

anchor.snippet: a weighted moving average is a convolution

annotations.note: The weighted moving average as a convolution application is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Distribution of sum of independent random variables

anchor.snippet: the probability distribution of the sum of two independent random variables is the convolution of their individual distributions

annotations.note: `IndepFun.map_add_eq_map_conv_map₀` proves the distribution of the sum of two independent random variables is the convolution of their distributions.

annotations.label: Voigt line shape

anchor.snippet: the line shape is a convolution of Gaussian and Lorentzian, a Voigt function

annotations.note: The Voigt profile (Gaussian–Lorentzian convolution) is not formalized in Mathlib.
